skill.name: writing-skills
skill.description: Use when creating, editing, or validating AI skills with test-driven documentation and pressure scenarios.
skill.summary: Applies test-driven development methods to writing and testing reusable AI skills.
skill.author: ZhanlinCui

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but most are false positives from Markdown examples, documentation links, and quoted file paths. The confirmed risk is the bundled render-graphs.js helper, which runs Graphviz and writes generated files under a user-supplied skill directory. No evidence found for malicious intent, credential exfiltration, command-and-control behavior, or prompt injection.

medium_findings.title: Executable Graphviz Helper Runs Local Commands
medium_findings.description: The render-graphs.js helper imports child_process and runs dot through execSync. The command string is fixed, so direct shell injection was not found, but it still executes a local external tool on DOT content extracted from a user-selected SKILL.md file.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script directly uses execSync for Graphviz execution. The command is hardcoded and receives data through stdin, which lowers injection risk but confirms external command execution.

medium_findings.title: Helper Reads and Writes Under a User-Supplied Directory
medium_findings.description: The helper resolves the skill directory from a command-line argument, reads SKILL.md, creates a diagrams directory, and writes SVG or DOT outputs. This is expected tool behavior, but users should only run it on trusted local skill directories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem behavior is explicit and semantically legitimate for a renderer. Risk remains because the path is user supplied and output files are created automatically.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Blocks Trigger Static Command Detections
low_findings.description: Many reported Ruby backtick and command findings are fenced examples in documentation, not executable code. These examples explain skill structure, shell snippets, or Markdown patterns and do not run by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are inside Markdown fences or prose examples. I found no code path that executes those snippets automatically.

low_findings.title: Documentation Image URLs Are Not Runtime Network Calls
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are image links in copied documentation. They may load if rendered by a Markdown viewer, but the skill code does not fetch them or send data to them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in Markdown links and image tags. No JavaScript or shell code performs network requests to those URLs.

low_findings.title: Home Directory Paths Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The hidden home directory findings reference example paths such as skill library locations. They describe where skills may live and do not access those paths directly.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The paths are shown as prose and command examples in documentation. I did not find automatic reads from hidden home files.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and C2 Keyword Hits Are Semantic False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and command-and-control detections appear to match words such as hash, md, command, and image query parameters in documentation. No evidence found of cryptographic implementation, encoded payloads, beaconing, or remote control logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context is authoring guidance and citations, not crypto code or network control logic. This is a strong false-positive pattern match.

low_findings.title: Persuasion Guidance Could Be Misused in Prompt Design
low_findings.description: The skill includes guidance on authority, commitment, and scarcity techniques for improving compliance. It frames the use as ethical skill design, but marketplace users should avoid using these techniques to bypass user intent or safety controls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text explicitly discusses increasing AI compliance, which has dual-use potential. The same file also includes an ethical-use section, so malicious intent is not confirmed.

dangerous_patterns.title: child_process.execSync Usage
dangerous_patterns.description: render-graphs.js uses execSync to invoke Graphviz. The command is fixed, but executing external binaries should remain visible to users.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The import and execSync calls are direct evidence of external process execution. The hardcoded command limits command-injection risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Synchronous Filesystem Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: render-graphs.js creates a diagrams directory and writes SVG or DOT files into the selected skill directory.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The fs.mkdirSync and fs.writeFileSync calls are explicit. The filenames are derived from constrained graph names, reducing path traversal risk.

content.value_statement: Unverified skills can fail when agents face pressure, shortcuts, or unclear instructions. This skill helps teams write, test, and refine AI skills using TDD-style scenarios.
actual_capabilities: Defines a TDD-style process for creating and editing AI skills.
Explains how to write trigger-focused skill descriptions and concise SKILL.md files.
Provides pressure scenario formats for testing agent compliance under realistic constraints.
Includes guidance for skill file organization, flowcharts, and supporting references.
Documents common rationalizations and ways to close instruction loopholes.
Provides a Graphviz helper for rendering DOT flowcharts from skill files.
limitations: Requires human judgment to design realistic pressure scenarios.
Assumes access to agents or subagents for testing behavior.
The Graphviz renderer requires a local dot installation.
Does not replace platform security review for published skills.

use_cases.title: Build a New Skill
use_cases.description: Create a concise skill with clear triggers, tested instructions, and focused supporting files.

use_cases.title: Improve a Weak Skill
use_cases.description: Use pressure scenarios to find where an agent skips, misreads, or rationalizes around the skill.

use_cases.title: Prepare Skills for Deployment
use_cases.description: Run a checklist that confirms structure, testing evidence, and deployment readiness before sharing a skill.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Skill
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a first skill draft.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a skill for this recurring workflow: [workflow]. Identify the trigger, core principle, required sections, and one pressure scenario.

prompt_templates.title: Test Skill Compliance
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request to verify behavior under pressure.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create three pressure scenarios for this skill: [skill summary]. Include time pressure, sunk cost, and authority pressure.

prompt_templates.title: Refine a Skill After Failure
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request after an agent ignored or bypassed guidance.
prompt_templates.prompt: The agent failed this scenario: [failure]. Analyze the rationalization and propose focused skill edits that close the loophole.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Deployment Readiness
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request before publishing or sharing a skill.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this skill for deployment readiness: [skill text]. Check triggers, testing evidence, file organization, limits, and common rationalizations.

output_examples.input: Create a skill for reviewing database migrations before release.
output_examples.output: A concise skill outline with a trigger-focused description, review checklist, pressure scenarios, and deployment checks.

output_examples.input: An agent keeps skipping the skill when the task seems simple.
output_examples.output: A rationalization table, stronger trigger wording, and targeted tests for simple but risky tasks.

output_examples.input: Validate my skill before publishing it to the team.
output_examples.output: A readiness review covering description quality, supporting files, test evidence, and unresolved limitations.

best_practices: Run a baseline scenario before writing or editing the skill.
Keep the description focused on when to use the skill, not the full process.
Capture exact agent rationalizations and address them directly.
anti_patterns: Writing a skill because the process feels obvious without testing failure modes.
Using the description as a long workflow summary that replaces the skill body.
Adding broad rules without tying them to observed failures.

faq.question: What does this skill help me write?
faq.answer: It helps create and improve AI skills that agents can discover, read, and follow under pressure.

faq.question: Does it generate a finished skill automatically?
faq.answer: It provides a process and checklist, but the author still designs scenarios and validates results.

faq.question: Why does it use test-driven development language?
faq.answer: It treats skill instructions like process code that must fail first, pass, and then be refactored.

faq.question: Can it be used with Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance is written for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code style skill workflows.

faq.question: Does the skill include executable code?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a local Graphviz renderer that reads SKILL.md files and writes diagram outputs.

faq.question: Should I publish skills after one successful test?
faq.answer: No. The workflow recommends repeated pressure testing until agents stop finding new rationalizations.
